message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 10
Recent Green phase: Added FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE, isFollowUp param to priceQuoteStep, runScenario passes index > 0.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the architecture notes and current implementation to evaluate refactoring opportunities.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: tightened `runScenario` return type from `{ results: unknown[] }` to `{ results: { premium: number }[] }`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — documents the shape tests exercise; `premium: number` field-name contract is now visible at the signature
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(scenario) -> { results: { premium: number }[] }`; field name `premium` confirmed (per example-mapping contract)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: extracted magic numbers in `runScenario` to module-level named constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1` and `PROCESSING_FEE = 5`
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — these are global pricing-rule constants that more upcoming tests will reference; naming them centralises and documents intent at every use site. Rule 2 trumps APP (mass +5).
11	- Naming established: `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` (10% surcharge on first-insurance, applied to policy base), `PROCESSING_FEE` (flat 5 G added to every quote), `BASE_PREMIUM` (lookup table by item type); per-step local names `policyBase` (sum of item base premiums) and `firstInsurance` (surcharge amount, not boolean flag)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: extracted per-step pricing into a private module-level function `priceQuoteStep(step: QuoteStep) -> { premium: number }`; `runScenario` is now just `scenario.steps.map(priceQuoteStep)`
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "price one quote step" concept that was previously anonymous inside `.map(...)`. Upcoming surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, follow-up) have a clear home and won't bloat `runScenario`. Mass roughly neutral.
16	- Naming established: `priceQuoteStep(step)` is the canonical per-step pricing helper; future surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty discount, follow-up discount) should compose inside this function, not in `runScenario`
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- Change: extracted `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` helpers; named the `0.5` magic number as `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`; renamed local `curseSurcharge` to `itemSurchargeTotal` (now an accumulator for all per-item surcharges, not just curse).
20	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup was duplicated across two reduces; now centralised in `itemBasePremium`. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `itemSurcharges(item)` is the natural extension point for upcoming high-enchantment (per-item) surcharge.
21	- Naming established: `itemBasePremium(item) -> number` (single source of base-premium lookup, will absorb rune-block logic later); `itemSurcharges(item) -> number` (sum of all per-item surcharges — add high-enchantment here next); `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`; the local accumulator name `itemSurchargeTotal` (NOT `curseSurcharge`) reflects that it sums all per-item surcharges.
22	
23	## After cycle 5
24	- Change: rewrote `itemSurcharges` from imperative `let surcharge = 0; if (…) surcharge += …` accumulator to two named expression bindings (`curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`) summed in the return; added constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
25	- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — eliminated 3 assignments and a mutable `let`, reducing APP mass ~43 → ~26 (Δ -17). Rule 2 (reveals intent) — each surcharge component is now a named value at the call site, so future surcharges added inside `itemSurcharges` follow a clear pattern: `const xSurcharge = condition ? base * X_RATE : 0`.
26	- Naming established: pattern for new per-item surcharges is a named binding `<name>Surcharge = condition ? base * <NAME>_RATE : 0`; the return is a flat sum of these bindings (no mutable accumulator). Constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
27	
28	## After cycle 6
29	- Change: introduced loyalty-discount logic in cycle 6's green phase (`LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`, applied to `policyBase` in `priceQuoteStep`); refactored by extracting a generic `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` helper and replacing the two duplicated `step.items.reduce((sum, x) => sum + f(x), 0)` call sites with `sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium)` and `sumOver(step.items, itemSurcharges)`.
30	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the "sum f(x) over a collection" pattern was duplicated. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `priceQuoteStep` now reads as a flat sequence of named pricing terms with no `reduce` noise; the per-step total mass dropped from ~44 to ~23. The helper will be reused for sum-of-payouts in upcoming claim tests.
31	- Naming established: `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` is the canonical "sum a function over a collection" helper — use it instead of inlining `xs.reduce((s, x) => s + f(x), 0)`. `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`; loyalty discount is applied to `policyBase` only (NOT to surcharges, fee, or first-insurance), inside `priceQuoteStep`.
32

content.text:
All 10 tests pass.

## Refactor Phase Analysis

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Looking at the newly modified `priceQuoteStep(step, yearsWithMHPCO, isFollowUp)`:

- **`priceQuoteStep`**: Still accurate — prices one quote step.
- **`isFollowUp`**: Clear boolean — does this step receive the follow-up discount?
- **`yearsWithMHPCO`**: Customer field name passed through; clear.
- **`followUpDiscount` / `FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE`**: Both reveal intent.

The signature `priceQuoteStep(step, yearsWithMHPCO, isFollowUp)` has grown to 3 parameters that are all customer/context bits being threaded through. There's a smell here: callers must compute `index > 0` and unpack `customer.yearsWithMHPCO`. A `context` parameter object (or just passing the `customer` plus `isFollowUp`) could reveal intent better. Let me weigh against actual benefit.

### Step 2: Initial APP Mass for `priceQuoteStep`

```
function priceQuoteStep(step, yearsWithMHPCO, isFollowUp): { premium: number }
  const policyBase = sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium);          // 2 bindings, 1 invocation
  const itemSurchargeTotal = sumOver(step.items, itemSurcharges);   // 1 binding, 1 invocation
  const firstInsurance = policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;         // 1 binding, 1 invocation (*)
  const loyaltyDiscount =
    yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS                       // 1 conditional, 1 invocation (>=)
      ? policyBase * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE                          // 1 invocation
      : 0;                                                          // 1 constant
  const followUpDiscount = isFollowUp                               // 1 conditional
    ? policyBase * FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE                          // 1 invocation
    : 0;                                                            // 1 constant
  const premium = Math.ceil(                                        // 1 invocation
    policyBase + itemSurchargeTotal + firstInsurance               // 5 invocations (+ - - +)
      - loyaltyDiscount - followUpDiscount + PROCESSING_FEE,
  );
  return { premium };
```

Rough count (just this function body):
- Constants: 2 (zeros) = 2
- Bindings: ~5 (policyBase, itemSurchargeTotal, firstInsurance, loyaltyDiscount, followUpDiscount, premium) + 3 params = ~8 → ~8
- Invocations: ~12 = 24
- Conditionals: 2 = 8
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0
- ≈ **42**

### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (tests pass)**: green.

**Rule 2 (reveals intent)**: Two parameters of `priceQuoteStep` are about the customer + their position in the visit. There's now an asymmetric naming pattern at the call site: `loyaltyDiscount` reads as `yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS` (testing eligibility on customer state), and `followUpDiscount` reads as `isFollowUp ?` (a pre-computed boolean). The asymmetry is a clarity issue — both are eligibility checks, but one is computed inside and one outside.

The cleanest move (per the established pattern from cycle 5: `<name>Discount = condition ? base * <NAME>_RATE : 0`) would be to compute `isFollowUp` symmetrically. But the index information must come from `runScenario` — it's the only place that knows the step's position. So `isFollowUp` is the right boundary; this is already idiomatic.

**Rule 3 (no duplication)**: I see a real conceptual duplication. Both `loyaltyDiscount` and `followUpDiscount` are: *"if condition, take a fraction of policyBase, else 0"*. This is the exact same shape repeated for these two discounts (and identical to the curse-surcharge pattern in `itemSurcharges`). A small helper `policyBaseDiscount(condition, rate)` could DRY this — but it would require closing over `policyBase`, adding an arrow function or extra parameter. The mass savings are minimal and the named bindings already reveal intent clearly. **Skip — Rule 2 (clarity) trumps Rule 3 here.**

**Rule 4 (fewest elements)**: The `+ itemSurchargeTotal + firstInsurance - loyaltyDiscount - followUpDiscount` chain in the `Math.ceil` is fine.

### A real, modest improvement worth doing

Looking again at `runScenario`: the call site `priceQuoteStep(step, scenario.customer.yearsWithMHPCO, index > 0)` has the customer field flattened into a primitive. The `priceQuoteStep` signature has grown from 1 → 3 params in two cycles. Per architecture-notes cycle 3: *"upcoming surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, follow-up) have a clear home and won't bloat `runScenario`."* The discounts have grown to where `priceQuoteStep` now needs both customer state AND positional state. The natural grouping is to pass the customer through as a single value (matching the `Scenario` shape) — but the only customer field used right now is `yearsWithMHPCO`, and inventing a wrapper type is premature.

Instead, the lower-risk Rule 2 improvement: the `Math.ceil(...)` at the bottom mixes two concerns — pricing arithmetic and rounding-in-MHPCO's-favor. The example-mapping spec explicitly calls out **rounding-up in MHPCO's favor** as a domain rule (see todo: *"rounded UP to 198 in MHPCO's favor"*). That rule deserves a name.

Refactoring: extract `Math.ceil` to a named function `roundUpInFavorOfOffice` and use it at the return site. This:
- Names a domain rule that the spec explicitly calls out
- Creates a clear home for the matching "round DOWN on payouts" helper that upcoming claim tests will need
- Pure clarity move; mass roughly neutral (+1 binding for function name, -1 invocation lost since we still invoke it)

### Step 4: Implement Refactoring

content.text:
All 10 tests still pass.

### Step 5: New APP Mass

Mass change at call site: was `Math.ceil(...)` (1 invocation), now `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(...)` (1 invocation) — neutral. The helper itself adds 1 binding (param `amount`) + 1 invocation (`Math.ceil`) = mass 3, but it's a tiny named domain helper. Net mass change: ~+3 (negligible). Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps this small APP cost — exactly the same trade-off justified in cycles 2 and 6.

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 2 (reveals intent): Extracted `Math.ceil` to `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` with a docstring naming the MHPCO domain rule the spec explicitly calls out ("rounded UP to 198 in MHPCO's favor").
- This creates the home for the matching `roundDownInFavorOfOffice` helper that upcoming claim-payout tests will need (the spec says payouts are "rounded down in MHPCO's favor": e.g. "350.5, rounded down to 350").
- Mass change: ~+3 (one tiny helper). Rule 2 trumps APP — consistent with prior cycles.
- Naming: `priceQuoteStep(step, yearsWithMHPCO, isFollowUp)` parameter set evaluated and kept; signature growth is justified by the asymmetric origin of each input (`yearsWithMHPCO` from customer state, `isFollowUp` from positional context — only `runScenario` can compute the latter).

### Step 7: Append to architecture-notes.md

input.old_string:
## After cycle 6
- Change: introduced loyalty-discount logic in cycle 6's green phase (`LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`, applied to `policyBase` in `priceQuoteStep`); refactored by extracting a generic `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` helper and replacing the two duplicated `step.items.reduce((sum, x) => sum + f(x), 0)` call sites with `sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium)` and `sumOver(step.items, itemSurcharges)`.
- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the "sum f(x) over a collection" pattern was duplicated. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `priceQuoteStep` now reads as a flat sequence of named pricing terms with no `reduce` noise; the per-step total mass dropped from ~44 to ~23. The helper will be reused for sum-of-payouts in upcoming claim tests.
- Naming established: `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` is the canonical "sum a function over a collection" helper — use it instead of inlining `xs.reduce((s, x) => s + f(x), 0)`. `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`; loyalty discount is applied to `policyBase` only (NOT to surcharges, fee, or first-insurance), inside `priceQuoteStep`.
input.new_string:
## After cycle 6
- Change: introduced loyalty-discount logic in cycle 6's green phase (`LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`, applied to `policyBase` in `priceQuoteStep`); refactored by extracting a generic `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` helper and replacing the two duplicated `step.items.reduce((sum, x) => sum + f(x), 0)` call sites with `sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium)` and `sumOver(step.items, itemSurcharges)`.
- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the "sum f(x) over a collection" pattern was duplicated. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `priceQuoteStep` now reads as a flat sequence of named pricing terms with no `reduce` noise; the per-step total mass dropped from ~44 to ~23. The helper will be reused for sum-of-payouts in upcoming claim tests.
- Naming established: `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` is the canonical "sum a function over a collection" helper — use it instead of inlining `xs.reduce((s, x) => s + f(x), 0)`. `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`; loyalty discount is applied to `policyBase` only (NOT to surcharges, fee, or first-insurance), inside `priceQuoteStep`.

## After cycle 7
- Change: introduced follow-up discount (`FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`, new `isFollowUp: boolean` param to `priceQuoteStep`, `runScenario` passes `index > 0`) in cycle 7's green phase; refactored by extracting `Math.ceil(...)` at the premium return into a named helper `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` with a docstring naming the MHPCO domain rule.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the spec explicitly calls out "rounded UP in MHPCO's favor" as a domain rule (visible in pending tests like "rounded UP to 198 in MHPCO's favor"); naming it pins that domain meaning at the call site instead of leaving a bare `Math.ceil`. Also opens the symmetric home for `roundDownInFavorOfOffice` that upcoming claim-payout tests require ("payout rounded DOWN in MHPCO's favor: 350.5 → 350"). Mass ~+3 — Rule 2 trumps APP, consistent with cycles 2/6.
- Naming established: `roundUpInFavorOfOffice(amount)` for quote premiums; the symmetric `roundDownInFavorOfOffice(amount)` (using `Math.floor`) is the expected home for claim-payout rounding. `FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15`; follow-up discount is applied to `policyBase` only (same scope as loyalty) inside `priceQuoteStep`. `isFollowUp: boolean` is computed by `runScenario` (`index > 0`) rather than inside `priceQuoteStep`, because positional context is only known at the scenario level — this asymmetry vs `yearsWithMHPCO` (customer field) is intentional and correct.
